summary: DHH argues that the discourse around Elon Musk's management of Twitter/X has devolved into pure motivated reasoning, with both supporters and detractors interpreting every development through their predetermined conclusions. He notes that countless predictions of Twitter's imminent demise have failed to materialize, yet critics never pause for introspection. Rather than engaging in the endless proxy war, DHH suggests people either enjoy the spectacle with detached amusement or tune out entirely. He acknowledges Musk may still fail but argues his survival through repeated supposed catastrophes demonstrates resilience.
key_points: Predictions of Twitter's collapse under Musk have repeatedly failed, yet critics never recalibrate their views
The discourse around Musk's decisions is a proxy war for ideological battles, not good-faith assessment
Both Musk's detractors and boosters engage in motivated reasoning, interpreting all evidence to confirm their priors
Surviving repeated supposed catastrophes demonstrates resilience, which is a key ingredient in success
The only rational responses are Camus-like detached amusement or tuning out entirely
money_quotes: Make sure you never miss a chance to be an expert on social media, advertising, team building, economics, or branding so you can tell the world's most successful entrepreneur what a total idiot he's being, and how much smarter, wiser, and kinder you'd be in his shoes.
However passionately you wish for the man to fail, he's not going to do so on account of your feeble bleating.
When you've survived so many supposed catastrophes, and you're still playing, you've at the very least shown tremendous resilience. That's one of the absolute key ingredients in success.
It's all turned into an endless proxy war, and every argument is wielded only in service of yet another petty ideological skirmish.
Musk could double the revenue and users of X, and his perma detractors would still find a path back to a prophesy of imminent ruin.
This is 5D chess being played by two parties shuffling pieces around in isolated realms of reality, each cheering every move as but two removed from checkmate.
Either embrace the absurdity of it all with the casual demeanor of Camus, and chuckle at the ever-grander spectacle, or tune out of the whole endeavor altogether.
key_insight: The debate over Musk's Twitter/X has become pure motivated reasoning on both sides, where no outcome could possibly change anyone's mind, making the entire discourse an exercise in competitive catastrophizing detached from reality.
